# Transcrição: 04- 4.0 - Hooking Your Audience With the 3 S's

- **Fonte:** 04- 4.0 - Hooking Your Audience With the 3 S's
- **Duração:** 15:39
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, I like to think at this point that you sort of have the skeleton of your story in place, which is to say, you know exactly the flow of your story, and you know all the parts, and you probably know the order that they're going to land in, although that can change. You may remove one, you may add a new scene, but you have an approximation of the skeleton of the story. It's now time to sort of fill that skeleton in. It's time to ensure that as you're speaking, the audience remains engaged in your story at all times. It's kind of a crazy thing, but when people stand on a stage or they're standing in a conference room, they're standing in front of a class of students or even in front of a congregation in a church. This crazy thing happens where people assume that because they're speaking, the audience is listening. I always assume that the audience wants to hear nothing I have to say, that when I take the stage, they kind of hate me and wish I would leave. That is the approach I take every single time. And because of that, I am relentless in my efforts to ensure that my audience wants to hear the next thing I say. I want to keep my audience in a perpetual state of wonder, which is to say, they're constantly wondering what will happen next. The best stories in the world achieve this goal. Twice in my life, I have gone to the movies and the power has gone out during the movie. The first time that happened, the manager came in and said, you can come back tomorrow or any other time and see the rest of the movie. We're so sorry. I did not go back to see the rest of that movie because I didn't care if the characters on the screen lived or died. I was not in a state of wonder when the power went out, so I never needed to see the end of that movie. The second time that happened, the power went out, my daughter was actually with me, Clara. And when the power went out, she sort of had a little panic attack. But once I got her under control and the manager came in and said the same thing, come back whenever you want, we were back the next day because I had to know what happened to the characters in that movie. That's the goal. If the power goes out, does the audience desperately want to hear the next sentence you were about to speak? If they are waiting for that sentence, you are achieving the goal. So you must constantly be thinking about, how can I get my audience to want to hear the next sentence in my story? I'm going to give you three ways that you're going to work on now. Three S's. The first S is stakes. What is it stake for the storyteller? What does the storyteller want or need? What is the danger that the storyteller is facing?

## [03:00 - 06:02]

What is the obstacle the storyteller must overcome? Those are stakes. They need to be placed immediately in a story. The audience has to be worried or wondering about what the future will be for the storyteller right from the jump. Within the first 30 seconds of a story, there has to be something that the audience is wondering about. In my story, the first thing they're wondering about is a little boy stuck in a tube. That's not actually what my story is about and you know that. My story is really about my daughter and about how my kids will eventually be able to take care of themselves, how they are already taking care of themselves. But I can't start with that. I can't place that stake in the beginning of my story because that's not going to come for a little while. So instead, I have to start with something that's going to engage my audience. That's why I'm not starting my story in the parking lot, walking into the McDonald's. There are no stakes involved there. I'm starting my story as close to the end as possible and I'm starting at a place where there are inherently stakes available to me. A little boy stuck in a tube and crying. The audience will begin wondering immediately how is Matt going to get his son out of the tube? Now that's not a huge stake. That is not an earth-shattering, terrifying stake, but it's something. It's a thing to begin with that gets the audience wondering. As the story goes on, I'm going to identify every time I can add a stake to the story. So another stake will come into play when I ask Clara to go into the tube and get Charlie and she refuses. That's a stake. That's a new stake. That is, Clara won't help. Will she change her mind? Will she help? We don't know what will happen. Another stake will happen when Clara is in that tube. And now I'm worried that she's going to get stuck because she refuses to cross over the cargo net. And now I have two kids stuck in a tube, a new stake. And by the way, did you notice I'm raising the stakes as I go along? You're getting worse and worse and worse. You see the same thing happen in books. You see the same thing happen in movies. The problem with a lot of storytellers is they like to take all of the stakes of their stories and pack them right in the front of a story. They pile them all up in fear that if they don't tell the audience everything to be worried about, the audience might not want to listen. We avoid that by starting with location and action and giving the audience something to worry about, a single stake, and then building stakes as we go along. When your anatomy of a story worksheet, you're going to place the stakes where you think they belong in the story. You're going to identify that first initial stake, even if it has nothing to do with the story, a false stake, something to get the audience to be worried about or wondering about, something to have the audience rooting for you, something that the audience wants you to overcome or wonders if you will overcome, something in the beginning of the story to

## [06:02 - 09:05]

get the audience invested in you and your journey. And then as you move along, identify additional stakes, hopefully building stakes along the way. The second way we're going to engage our audience is with suspense. Every story can have suspense. You do not need to be in the midst of a horror movie to create suspense because suspense is merely saying something, but not the whole thing. Suspense is very simple. You say to the audience, a few of the qualities of a thing while leaving out the rest of the thing. A simple way I'm going to do it in my story is I'm going to open with something like this. I'm standing below a tube in a McDonald's play place and there's a small boy about 12 feet over my head and he's crying. I've created some suspense right away by not telling you certain things. You don't know who the boy is yet. He's my son Charlie, but you don't know that. You don't know who the boy is. So you're wondering who the boy is. That's suspense. I'll look up and I'll say something like he's three or four years old, which is accurate. That's about how old I think he was back then, but I'm still not going to say his name and I'm not going to identify him as my son. I'm going to continue to get you to wonder about who the boy is and then I will reveal he's my son. His name is Charlie. Now that is not the kind of suspense that you'll see in a horror movie. It's not the kind of suspense that builds in the movie Jaws, but it is suspense, which means I've taken that boy, my son Charlie, in a tube and I've told you something about him, but not the whole thing. And then I'm going to tell you something more about him, but not the whole thing. And then a little bit more, but not the whole thing. And then I will reveal who he is. I'm going to do that throughout my story. I'm going to try to introduce a new character or a new place or a new thing. I'm going to ask myself, can I say a little bit of that thing first and then say the rest? Because that's what suspense is. Go through your story and ask yourself, are there places in my story where characters are introduced, places are introduced, objects are introduced, and I can say some of it, but not the whole thing. Tell a story about a spoon. And the moment the spoon appears in the hands of a fifth grader, I say, I see a metal object in the boy's hand. I don't say it's a spoon. And I choose my words carefully. I see a metal object in a boy's hand. No one is thinking spoon at that point. At best, they're wondering what is in his hand. At worst, they're thinking knife or gun or something dangerous. But all of those are fantastic for me.

## [09:05 - 12:08]

Whether your audience is wondering about what the thing is or they're guessing about what the thing is, both of those things represent suspense. And if you're creating suspense, big suspense, how will Matt get the kids out of the tube that's big suspense and small suspense? Who's the boy in the tube crying? Do you know the boy? All of those little moments and those big moments will contribute to engaging your audience and getting them to continue to be invested in your story. The third one you're going to be looking for is surprise. And it's my favorite. It is the most powerful thing you have to offer as a storyteller. Surprise is the thing that everyone remembers about a story. It's the moment when their stomach flips, their heart breaks, their mind opens. It is the moment in the usual suspects when you discover that verbal is actually Kaiser so say it's the moment in the sixth sense when you discover my God, Bruce Willis was dead all along. It's the moment in fight club when you realize the two men are actually one man. Those are moments in cinema that you never forget because they're so surprising. Storytellers have the opportunity to surprise their audience and create an enormous emotional reaction and unforgettable emotional reaction. And that's what I'm going to be going for in every story that I tell in every story has surprise. Every story has at least one surprise. The one surprise is your five second moment because every time you suddenly see the world in a new way, did you hear the word I used, which is a terrible storytelling word suddenly suddenly I see the world in a new way that suddenly word is a signal that it's surprising. I used to think one thing and then some stuff happened and there was a singular moment when my mind changed and that is surprising to you. And therefore you should make it surprising to your audience. That's the trick of surprise. You have to ask yourself, when was I surprised over the course of my story? And when I was surprised, my audience should also be surprised. I know the moments of surprise in my story. I know the first moment of surprise for me is the moment when Clara gets up and walks to that tower and begins climbing. I never thought she was going to do it. As a result, as a storyteller, my job is to convince you she's never going to do it because that was my mindset that day. So it needs to be your mindset. And when I reveal that she's going to go over to that tower and start climbing, I have to reveal it in such a way that is surprising to you by reproducing that moment exactly for you as I witnessed it.

## [12:08 - 15:14]

That's a moment of surprise that I need to maximize. I need to focus on it and make sure I pull it off perfectly. Another moment of surprise is my five second moment. The moment Clara says, Charlie, speak the name of the person you love most and that will give you the courage you need. I'll never forget that moment. It was utterly and completely shocking to me. I want the audience to feel the same thing. And so I need to make sure that when I get to that moment, those words are spoken in such a way that you can't believe they happened either. Those are two moments of surprise that I know are going to take place in my story. Two moments that I have to make sure I craft carefully. I protect and preserve. I make sure that nothing gives those moments away. On your anatomy of a story worksheet, I want you to identify moments of surprise and moments of possible suspense and mark them in the worksheet. There's a place to do it. Find the moments when you were surprised and then you're going to say to yourself, this is now where my audience must also be surprised. What can I do to surprise them? There's a multitude of strategies I have to help people create surprise and stories. What I'm going to offer to you is simply this, identify the location where surprise is going to take place. You produce that moment exactly the way that you witnessed it and don't speak of anything in the story that will give that moment away. Those are three things, three of many, but those will take you a long way. So before you move on to the next video, you're going to identify the stakes of your story and mark them on your worksheet. Hopefully, build those stakes over time. You're going to mark moments of suspense when people or objects or places come into your story and you have the chance to speak about some of it before speaking about all of it. And then moments of surprise. Where was I surprised? When do I remember being surprised? How can I make my audience feel that same level of surprise? Mark it on your worksheet and then begin thinking about how you're going to pull off each one of them. Start speaking it out loud if you need to, write it down if you need to. These are critical aspects of storytelling. These are the things that get people to continue to listen to you as you speak. We cannot assume as storytellers that anyone wants to hear anything we have to say. I understand why people don't think this way because once again, you have told 99.999% of the stories you've ever told in your entire life to people who love you and want to hear your stories. But that doesn't mean you've been telling effective stories. You've had an audience that is already engaged because they like you and care about you.

## [15:14 - 15:39]

Storytellers like me, storytellers who want to speak to people who don't know you, we have to relentlessly find ways to keep people caring about the words we're saying. And three of the best ways you can do it is stakes, suspense, and surprise. Go see what you can do with those three elements now. And when you're ready, go on to the next video.
